title: Statistical and Algorithmic Foundations of Probing Quantum Systems with Compressive Measurements: A Review
authors: Zhen Qin, Michael B. Wakin, Zhihui Zhu
abstract: Quantum state tomography (QST) is a fundamental task in quantum information science that aims to reconstruct unknown quantum states from measurement data. However, the exponential growth of Hilbert-space dimension with system size makes full tomography of general quantum states statistically and computationally prohibitive. This challenge has motivated extensive research on structured quantum state tomography, where prior structure, such as low-rankness, tensor-network representations, shallow quantum circuits, and neural quantum states, can substantially reduce the effective degrees of freedom and enable scalable recovery. In this review, we provide a unified perspective on QST for structured quantum states through three closely related themes: compact state representations, measurement design, and computational algorithms. After reviewing common models for structured quantum states, we survey existing work on geometric preservation properties of measurement frameworks, ranging from informationally complete POVMs to randomized measurements, and their implications for sample complexity. On the algorithmic side, we review optimization methods for reconstructing structured quantum states from empirical measurements. By connecting QST with broader principles from compressive sensing, matrix sensing, and structured inverse problems, this survey highlights common theoretical foundations underlying sample complexity, measurement efficiency, and scalable recovery.
summary: This review provides a unified perspective on performing quantum state tomography on large-scale systems by exploiting structural priors. It connects the theory of measurement design and optimization algorithms to the field of compressed sensing, showing how to reduce the complexity of state reconstruction from exponential to polynomial scaling.
main_problem: The exponential scaling of Quantum State Tomography (QST) makes reconstructing general quantum states computationally and statistically impossible for large systems, necessitating methods that exploit inherent state structures.
main_result: The review establishes a unified framework for QST by connecting compact state representations, measurement design, and reconstruction algorithms, demonstrating that QST can be treated as a structured inverse problem.
method: The paper surveys various approaches including compressive sensing, randomized measurements (Haar-random, t-designs, and classical shadows), and optimization-based reconstruction (convex relaxations and non-convex methods).
model_or_system: The review focuses on large-scale quantum processors, programmable simulators, and error-corrected computers, specifically targeting structured states such as low-rank, tensor-network (MPS, MPO, PEPO), and neural quantum states.
key_observables: Fidelities, expectation values of Pauli observables, trace distance, and entanglement measures.
important_parameters: Hilbert-space dimension (d), number of measurement shots (M), number of POVMs (Q), and intrinsic dimension/complexity of the state.
main_assumptions: Assumes the existence of underlying structure (e.g., low-rankness or tensor-network compressibility) and focuses on the statistical error from finite-shot measurements rather than hardware-specific noise.
paper_structure: The paper is organized around three interconnected themes: compact state representations, measurement design, and computational algorithms, framing QST as a structured inverse problem.
why_interesting: It provides a comprehensive theoretical bridge between signal processing (compressive sensing) and quantum information, offering scalable strategies for characterizing many-body dynamics and large-scale quantum devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustration of the MPO in (30) from two perspectives: (a) each entry of the density matrix can be represented as products of n matrices, where green represents one entry and the corresponding n matrices, and (b) each element of the density matrix is illustrated in a diagrammatic form, where the line connecting two circles signifies the tensor contraction operation [96], and unconnected line segments denote indices.
